data.title: Stryker Q4 results beat on both lines; 2026 EPS guidance includes consensus
data.description:
• Stryker (SYK) is up ~2% in after-hours trading Thursday after posting Q4 financial results that beat on both lines and issued 2026 EPS guidance range that includes the consensus.
  • In 2026, the medtech sees non-GAAP EPS of $14.90 to $15.10. Consensus is $14.94.
  • The company also sees organic net sales growth this year of 8%-9.5%.
  • Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $4.47 compares to $4.01 in the year-ago period. 
  • Stryker was helped in the quarter by 17.5% year-over-year sales growth in its MedSurg and Neurotechnology business. The Orthopaedics division increased by a more modest 2.2%.
  • The company ended the year with ~$4B in cash and cash equivalents compared to ~$3.7B on Dec. 31, 2024.

data.title: GrubHub going after rivals by removing fees on orders over $50
data.description:
In move that hopes to increase order sizes and ensure customer loyalty, GrubHub is removing service and delivery fees on restaurant orders over $50.

The new policy will begin Thursday in a limited scope with a broader launch slated for February 2.

With free delivery already offered to paid subscribers, the privately owned GrubHub hopes that by extending free delivery to orders over $50, consumers will switch from DoorDash (DASH) and Uber Eats (UBER) both of which charge a delivery fee of as much as $8 along with a 10% to 15% service fee. Additionally, Uber Eats (UBER) charges another $2 for orders below $10.

And by eliminating delivery charges over $50, GrubHub hopes to avoid “checkout abandonment,” in which customers drop large orders with high delivery fees.

With a dwindling market share of just 4% versus 71% for DoorDash (DASH) and 23% for Uber Eats (UBER), GrubHub aims to lure in new customers, encourage larger orders, and eliminate the “biggest pain point” in the food delivery industry, GrubHub CEO Howard Midgal said in an interview with Bloomberg.  

The news briefly weighed on shares of Uber Technologies (UBER) and DoorDash (DASH), though both have recovered modest losses and were higher at Thursday’s close.

data.title: Apple edges up after Q1 revenue jumps 16% on record iPhone sales
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) shares edged higher during early post-market trading on Thursday after releasing its first quarter fiscal 2026 financial results that were driven by record-breaking iPhone sales.

For the quarter ended December 31, Apple reported earnings per share of $2.84 versus the consensus estimate of $2.67.

Revenue for the quarter increased 15.7% to total $143.76B, which was more than the $138.52B estimate. Apple reported double-digit revenue growth across multiple business segments. Product revenue increased 16% to $107.7B, which was led by iPhone revenue surging 23% to $85.27B. Services revenue climbed 16% year over year to $30B. 

Apple also received a bump from China, with sales growing by 38% year over year to $25.5B. The Americas led all regions with 11% revenue growth to $58.5B. 

"iPhone had its best-ever quarter driven by unprecedented demand, with all-time records across every geographic segment, and Services also achieved an all-time revenue record, up 14 percent from a year ago," said Apple CEO Tim Cook. "We are also excited to announce that our installed base now has more than 2.5 billion active devices, which is a testament to incredible customer satisfaction for the very best products and services in the world."

"During the December quarter, our record business performance and strong margins led to EPS growth of 19 percent, setting a new all-time EPS record," added Apple Chief Financial Officer. Kevan Parekh, Apple’s CFO. "These exceptionally strong results generated nearly $54 billion in operating cash flow, allowing us to return almost $32 billion to shareholders."

"Apple reported its best quarter ever on Thursday afternoon, smashing street estimates for both revenues and earnings per share," said Seeking Alpha analyst Bill Maurer. "Total revenues grew more than 15.6% thanks to a more than 23.3% surge in iPhone revenues, fueling strong margin improvement. The company generated a whopping $51.55 billion in free cash flow during the all-important holiday quarter, nearly double the year-ago period, sending shares higher in the after-hours session."

Apple's board of directors also declared a cash dividend of $0.26 per share of the company's common stock.

data.title: OR Royalties buys additional NSR royalty on Ghana gold mine in $103M deal
data.description:
OR Royalties (OR) said Thursday it acquired an additional 1% net smelter return royalty covering the Namdini gold mine in Ghana, obtaining Savannah Mining's remaining 50% interest in the 2% NSR royalty, for total consideration of as much as $103.5M.

OR Royalties (OR) said it made an initial $28.5M payment upon closing with an additional $70M payment upon certain milestones, which are expected to occur over the next two quarters.

Gold production at Namdini began in H1 2025, with the mine in the final phases of a ramp-up towards peak output of ~360K oz/year over the first three years as well as an expected average of 287K oz/year of gold over an initial 15-year life of mine.

The Namdini mine is an open-pit gold mine operated by Shandong Gold (SDGMF).

data.title: Civista signals mid-single-digit loan growth and expanding net interest margin in 2026 while integrating Farmers Savings Bank
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Civista Bancshares, Inc. (CIVB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Dennis Shaffer, President, CEO, and Chairman, reported that "net income for the fourth quarter of 2025 of $12.3 million or $0.61 per diluted share, which is consistent with our linked quarter and represents a $2.4 million or 24% increase over the fourth quarter in 2024." He highlighted nonrecurring expenses related to the Farmers Savings Bank acquisition, which impacted net income by $3.4 million pretax and $2.9 million after tax, equating to $0.14 per common share. Shaffer also noted that "For the year, we reported net income of $46.2 million or $2.64 per diluted share which compares to $31.7 million or $2.01 per diluted share for 2024."
  • The CEO announced the completion of the Farmers Savings Bank acquisition, adding $106 million in loans and $236 million in low-cost deposits. He stated, "Excluding the newly acquired Farmers loans, our loan and lease portfolio grew $68.7 million, which represents an annualized growth rate of 8.7% during the fourth quarter."
  • Shaffer underscored a dividend increase to $0.18 per share and the maintenance of a $13.5 million share repurchase authorization. He emphasized, "We believe our stock is a value, and we will continue to evaluate repurchase opportunity."
  • Ian Whinnem, Interim Principal Accounting Officer, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Net interest income for the quarter totaled $36.5 million which is $1.9 million or a 5.5% increase over the linked quarter and a $5.1 million or 16% increase over our fourth quarter and the previous year."

Outlook

  • Management anticipates "mid-single-digit loan growth in 2026" and expects additional expenses related to the Farmers Savings Bank transaction to be minimal going forward.
  • Shaffer indicated the company will "remain mindful of making sure we have the funding and capital to support future CRE growth" and aims to maintain a loan-to-deposit ratio within the targeted 90% to 95% range.
  • The company expects to leverage Farmers' excess deposits and expanding loan pipelines to support organic growth.
  • Whinnem provided margin guidance, stating, "for the first quarter, we'd expect that margin to expand 2 to 3 basis points and then into the second quarter and beyond, maybe another 3 to 4 and capping out around there," assuming a rate cut in June and again in the fourth quarter.

Financial Results

  • Net interest margin expanded by 11 basis points to 3.69% for the quarter, as earning asset yield declined 8 basis points and funding costs declined 19 basis points.
  • Noninterest income increased $251,000 from the linked quarter and $869,000 from Q4 2024, driven by higher interchange and leasing fees.
  • After adjusting for $3.4 million in nonrecurring acquisition expenses, noninterest expense was $27.6 million, nearly flat with the prior quarter.
  • Efficiency ratio improved to 57.7% from 61.4% in the linked quarter and 68.3% in the prior year.
  • Credit quality remained solid, with nonperforming loans rising by $8.5 million to $31.3 million, primarily from one isolated credit. Allowance for credit losses to total loans was 1.28% at year-end.

Q&A

  • Justin Crowley, Piper Sandler, inquired about the composition of loan growth and impact of digital channels. Charles Parcher responded, "the commercial area will leave that growth, both C&I and commercial real estate," and digital channels are expected to accelerate growth but are in the early stages.
  • Crowley asked about margin trends; Whinnem replied, "for the first quarter, we'd expect that margin to expand 2 to 3 basis points... assuming a cut in June and then again in the fourth quarter."
  • On expenses post-acquisition, Whinnem explained, "first quarter expenses to be similar to where we are maybe in that 29% range... In the second quarter, we're going to have the merit increases."
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson, asked about credit quality. Michael Mulford clarified, "the credit quality we brought over from FSB was very good... We had 1 credit that we had a participation with another bank that we put on nonaccrual in the fourth quarter, it was about $8 million."
  • Terence McEvoy, Stephens, asked about loan yields. Parcher noted, "we put last December new and renew came on at 6.7%," and competition remains robust in Ohio and Indiana.
  • Timothy Switzer, KBW, asked about capital optimization and repurchases. Shaffer stated, "We still think we're a value. So we continue to -- we didn't repurchase anything last year, but we do have our $13.5 million authorization in place."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth drivers, credit quality, digital strategy, and expense trajectory, with a neutral to slightly positive tone and some pressing on digital and margin specifics.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing strategic investments, successful integration, and their confidence in future growth. Shaffer's remarks, such as "I remain confident that this quarter and this year... positions us very well for long-term future success," reflected this stance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained confidence, but with more clarity and detail about post-acquisition integration and forward loan growth guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from mid- to high-single-digit loan growth (Q3) to a more measured "mid-single-digit" outlook for 2026.
  • Management’s tone remained confident, but with increased specificity regarding integration and digital initiatives compared to the prior quarter.
  • Efficiency ratio and noninterest expense improvements were highlighted in Q4, whereas Q3 focused on capital raise and payoffs.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from acquisition close and capital raise in Q3 to operational execution and digital channel ramp in Q4.
  • Nonperforming loans increased this quarter due to a single credit, but overall credit quality and allowance remained stable.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged the unpredictability of leasing fees and residual income, noting, "leasing fees, particularly residual income are less predictable than more traditional banking fees."
  • Past due loans increased $7 million, and nonperforming loans rose $8.5 million, attributed to one participation loan.
  • Integration of Farmers Savings Bank and successful system conversion are key operational risks in the near term.
  • Competition in Ohio and Indiana lending markets remains high, with management closely watching loan repricing and funding costs.

Final Takeaway

Civista Bancshares’ Q4 2025 call emphasized steady fundamental improvement, successful acquisition integration, and prudent capital deployment, with management reiterating a focus on disciplined loan growth, expense management, and leveraging technology and digital channels for future expansion. The company projects mid-single-digit organic loan growth in 2026, expects net interest margin to expand modestly, and remains committed to maintaining strong credit metrics while optimizing its funding mix and capital base.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: PulteGroup outlines 3%–5% community count growth for 2026 amid strategic divestiture and margin focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: PulteGroup, Inc. (PHM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ryan Marshall highlighted that "PulteGroup's 2025 operating and financial results further demonstrate the value of our differentiated operating model that emphasizes diversification and balance across markets, buyer groups and spec versus build-to-order production as well as a highly disciplined approach to project underwriting and overall capital allocation." Marshall emphasized closing over 29,500 homes and generating home sale revenues of $16.7 billion for the year, with gross and operating margins of 26.3% and 16.9%, respectively. He noted that the company ended the year with $2 billion in cash after investing $5.2 billion into the business and returning $1.4 billion to shareholders. Marshall announced a strategic shift: "we have made the strategic decision to divest of our off-site manufacturing operations. ICG has proven to be a strong operator... but we have determined that our business and in turn, our shareholders are best served by us focusing on our core homebuilding operations."

  • CFO James Ossowski stated, "We recorded net new orders in the fourth quarter of 6,428 homes, which is an increase of 4% over Q4 of last year." He also reported fourth quarter home sale revenues of $4.5 billion, a 5% decrease from the prior year, and noted a decrease in absorption pace and an increase in incentives as the company worked to clear finished spec inventory. Ossowski emphasized, "We currently expect to realize gross margins of 24.5% to 25.0% for both the first quarter and for the full year of 2026."

Outlook

  • The company guided to closings between 5,700 and 6,100 homes for Q1 2026 and 28,500 to 29,000 homes for the full year. Average sales price of closings is expected to be in the range of $550,000 to $560,000 for both Q1 and full year 2026. Management expects average community count to be 3% to 5% higher than the comparable quarter of 2025. Gross margins are expected to remain in the 24.5% to 25.0% range for both Q1 and the full year, with house costs in 2026 projected to be flat to slightly down and lot costs to increase by 7% to 8%. SG&A expense for 2026 is projected at 9.5% to 9.7% of home sale revenue.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter net income was $502 million or $2.56 per share, down from $913 million or $4.43 per share in Q4 2024. For the full year, net income was $2.2 billion or $11.12 per share. Fourth quarter home sale revenues totaled $4.5 billion, reflecting a 3% decrease in closings and a 1% decrease in average sales price to $573,000. The company reported a gross margin of 24.7% for Q4 (including $35 million in land impairment charges) and incurred a charge of $81 million related to the divestiture of off-site manufacturing operations. Cash flow from operations for 2025 totaled $1.9 billion. Repurchases in Q4 amounted to 2.4 million common shares for $300 million.

Q&A

  • John Lovallo, UBS: Asked about SG&A levers and the ICG divestiture. Ryan Marshall explained targeted reductions in force in slower markets and said, "we're better off focusing on the core competency, buying land, entitling, developing, building homes."
  • Michael Rehaut, JPMorgan: Asked about gross margin outlook components. Marshall detailed, "Our expectations are really to see ASP flat through the year...our house costs to go down slightly...land cost to increase in the range of 7% to 8%."
  • Sam Reid, Wells Fargo: Queried about Q4 incentive load and spec inventory. Ossowski said, "The increase in the fourth quarter really was the incentives to move some of the speculative inventory." Marshall added, "our expectation is incentives will remain elevated."
  • Stephen Kim, Evercore ISI: Asked about spec levels and incentive assumptions. Marshall responded, "Ideally, what we're really endeavoring to do is to move back more into a build-to-order builder, where 60-plus percent of our sales are built-to-order, 40% are spec."
  • Alan Ratner, Zelman & Associates: Inquired about forward commitments and margin differential. Marshall said, "we have in the hundreds of basis points higher gross margins when it's built-to-order."
  • Anthony Pettinari, Citi: Sought details on impairment charges and build-to-rent exposure. Ossowski explained, "we had 8 of them that we took a land impairment charge on...we've been more disciplined as we've been looking at it."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were generally constructive but pressed on SG&A management, incentive trends, gross margin sustainability, and inventory strategy. There was persistent questioning on incentive levels and margin guidance, with a slightly cautious tone.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing discipline and operational strength, but shifted to a more cautious optimism and transparency in response to Q&A, especially regarding market uncertainties and incentives. Phrases such as "we'd hope and we'd be optimistic that we can pull back just a tad on those discounts" and "it's too early to glean much in terms of the strength of the entire spring selling season other than to say we remain optimistic" reflect this.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence remained strong in prepared remarks but was more tempered during Q&A, and analyst sentiment moved from neutral to slightly questioning on execution and margin durability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from holding on formal 2026 guidance in Q3 to providing detailed targets for 2026 in Q4. The company announced the divestiture of off-site manufacturing, a new strategic shift not mentioned previously. Management indicated continued focus on buyer and market diversification, but with greater emphasis on returning to a build-to-order model and managing spec inventory. Gross margin guidance for 2026 is lower than the Q4 2024 result. Analysts in Q4 were more focused on incentives, margin sustainability, and capital allocation, compared to Q3's focus on market stabilization and land pipeline. Management displayed slightly less certainty about incentive reduction in the near term and was more explicit about the impact of market variability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted variable buyer demand, affordability pressures, and regional softness in Texas and Western markets. Land inflation, projected at 7% to 8%, and elevated incentives were acknowledged as ongoing challenges. The company noted an $81 million charge related to the divestiture of off-site manufacturing operations and $35 million in Q4 land impairment charges. Market participants are closely watching the spring selling season and consumer confidence as potential risks to 2026 performance. Management emphasized a disciplined approach to land investment and inventory management as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

PulteGroup closed 2025 with strong operational and financial results, supported by a disciplined business model and diversification across markets and buyer groups. The company has set clear guidance for 2026, targeting 3% to 5% community count growth and stable gross margins, while divesting non-core operations to sharpen focus on homebuilding. Management remains optimistic, but acknowledges the importance of consumer confidence, affordability, and regional demand trends as key factors shaping future performance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Slapped with €7B lawsuit from Italian steel firm, ArcelorMittal hits back with €1.8B claim
data.description:
ArcelorMittal (MT) has filed a €1.8B (~$2.2B) lawsuit against the Italian government over losses linked to its investment in Italian steel firm Acciaierie d'Italia, Reuters reported Thursday, in a tit-for-tat move after Italy's state‑appointed administrators of ADI filed a lawsuit seeking ~€7B ($8.37B) in damages from ArcelorMittal, alleging that it had mismanaged ADI's steelworks.

ArcelorMittal (MT) said it saw "no factual nor legal basis" in the summons it was served by ADI's government-appointed commissioners to appear before a Milan court, and rejected all allegations, including assertions that it has pursued a strategy of running down the plants, destroying ADI's business and extracting profits from Italy.

The company (MT) said it had invested ~€2B "to turn around a structurally challenged business," much of which was devoted to meeting environmental standards set by the government.

ADI's future has become a major political issue for Prime Minister Meloni, since a shutdown would have significant ripple effects across Italy's manufacturing sector.

data.title: Lexicon Pharmaceuticals launches underwritten public stock offering
data.description:
• Lexicon Pharmaceuticals (LXRX) commenced an underwritten public offering of common stock.
  • The company plans to grant underwriters a 30-day option to purchase additional shares.
  • Concurrently, Lexicon plans a private placement of common stock to an affiliate of Invus, L.P., its largest stockholder, under its preemptive rights.
  • The private placement includes an option to purchase additional shares on a pro rata basis if underwriters exercise their option.
  • Proceeds from the offering are intended to fund ongoing R&D, working capital, and general corporate purposes.
  • LXRX shares down 2.8% post-market.
